titlestmt.titleproper: United States gay male leather culture collection7872

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2020-06-03 00:11:43 -0400.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: United States gay male leather culture collection
did.abstract: A collection of posters, handbills, buttons and other ephemera from the gay male leather subculture in San Francisco and other major American cities, ranging from the 1960s through to the early 1990s, and encompassing the post-Stonewall, pre-AIDs era, regarded as the 'Golden Age' of leather bars.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: A collection of posters, handbills, buttons and other ephemera from the gay male leather subculture in San Francisco and other major American cities, ranging from the 1960s through to the early 1990s, and encompassing the post-Stonewall, pre-AIDs era, regarded as the 'Golden Age' of leather bars.

The collection provides a vivid document of hypermasculine homoerotic iconography, represented in promotional materials for leather bars, sex hardware merchants and motorcycle clubs, and gathers together a diverse array of items, including more than 35 posters, 80 or so handbills and cards, approximately 150 buttons and pins, over 100 matchbooks, and a vintage leather biker's cap adorned with a colourful assortment of buttons dating from the late 1960s through to the early 1980s.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The 'Golden Age' of leather bars was 1969-1980, post-Stonewall, pre-AIDs era.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: United States gay male leather culture collection, #7872. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

did.unittitle: Series I. Handbills and Advertisements
Business card for the Why Not

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The first San Francisco bar to openly target leatherman patrons. It was opened by Tony Tavarossi in 1962 at 518 Ellis Street in the Tenderloin, but closed within six months after Tavarossi propositioned a member of the vice squad. Its success inspired a new generation of bars, many of which opened up in the South of Market district. In 1981 Tavarossi became an early fatality of the AIDS epidemic.

did.unittitle: Business card for the Why Not
Business card for the Blue Bugle Bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stroudsburg, PA. The card's verso prints a Handkerchief Guide. Donald A. 'Hans' Hunsicker opened the Blue Bugle Bar and Restaurant in 1970.

did.unittitle: seven colour slides of photographs taken at a leather motorcycle club gathering

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Weekend rides to picnic grounds in popular destinations such as the Sierra Nevada were regular events during the 1970s and 1980s.

did.unittitle: collection of matchbooks from various leather bars and clubs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Most originating from San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Matchbooks

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chicago. The modern bandana code, also known as the handkerchief or hanky code, is thought to have come into use among leather men in New York City in late 1970 or early 1971. The Gold Coast, the first specifically gay leather bar in America, opened in 1958.

did.unittitle: The Gold Coast Bandana

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Boot Camp opened as a uniform themed leather bar at 1010 Bryant St. in August 1971, receiving a mention in Herb Caen's San Francisco Chronicle column. Its manager, Marcus Hernandez (aka Mr. Marcus), was selected as the first gay Emperor of San Francisco in 1972.

did.unittitle: Boot Camp First Anniversary invite

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3520 16th St., San Francisco. The Shed opened at 1973 Market Street in 1972 and closed in 1977. The entrance to the club was originally at 3520 16th Street, and the large dance floor was in the basement.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Pearl with Stallion and Hot Water at The Shed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: UC Berkeley

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Gay Dance at Stephens Hall

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Stud was originally located at the western end of Folsom Street and opened on May 27th 1966. It was known for themed parties, drag and burlesque shows.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing live bluegrass at The Stud

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Illustrated throughout with drawings by Russ of Frisco.

did.unittitle: Catalogue for A Taste of Leather

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: UC Berkeley

did.unittitle: Handbill for a Gay Dance at the Pauley Ballroom

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Los Angeles. ONE Magazine began publication in Los Angeles in January 1953, followed three years later by the ONE Institute for Homophile Studies.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Activities of Interest to a Variety of Gays at the ONE Center

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Bojangles was a bar in San Francisco's Tenderloin district that operated between 1974 and 1977.

did.unittitle: Handbill for a dance at the New Bojangles

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Ambush leather bar opened at 1351 Harrison St. in November 1973, initially attracting a leather hippie crowd.

did.unittitle: Handbill for Ambush

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3010 North Broadway, Chicago

did.unittitle: Membership card for the Hi-Ho Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 different. San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Ambush business cards

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: A Taste of Leather - Febe's printed slip

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Book of 8 unused A Taste of Leather - Febe's detachable printed slips

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. An early show by The Cycle Sluts, an LA band of ten gender bending leather men who wore female S&M leather gear and performed heavy metal pastiche.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing The Cycle Sluts at the California Hall

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 315 Bush St., San Francisco. Sutter's Mill opened in 1965 on Kearny Street and moved to Bush Street in 1967.

did.unittitle: Please Don't Touch! Gone to Pee. A card from Sutter's Mill

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 315 Bush St., San Francisco, c. 1976. Sutter's Mill opened in 1965 on Kearny Street and moved to Bush Street in 1967.

did.unittitle: SM ads

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: SM ads was started in 1972 by Marshall Loeb as a nationwide service offering gay male subscribers a chance to place and respond to explicitly SM personal ads.

did.unittitle: SM ads
SM ads
SM ads
San Francisco Trucking Co. handbill

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 28 Ninth St., New York City. Illustration by Rex.

did.unittitle: Handbill for the Manhole Levi-leather club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chicago. The Closet was opened by two women in 1978 in Chicago's Boystown district.

did.unittitle: Drinks mat, The Closet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Los Angeles. Illustrated with a drawing by Tom. Eons, which held its first show in March 1976, was one of the earliest gay galleries, along with Fey-Way in San Francisco and Stompers in New York City.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing an exhibition of work by Tom of Finland at Eons

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Gay Freedom Day Sheet Music

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Screening Room opened in a converted storefront in 1967 and was one of the first theatres in San Francisco to show hardcore gay porn.

did.unittitle: Membership card, The Screening Room

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York City. Artwork by Boris Vallejo. The New St. Marks Baths, in St. Marks Place, was a bathhouse in the East Village from 1979 to 1985. It claimed to be the largest gay bathhouse in the world and was an early target of attention during the early 1980s AIDS epidemic.

did.unittitle: Handbill for The New St. Marks Baths

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 374 Fifth St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Card announcing After Carnival Ball at the Hothouse

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Programme for the First Annual Mr. Castro Pageant

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Printed Card. Stores such as Image Leather and The Trading Post promoted handkerchiefs and bandanas by printing cards such as this one listing the meanings of various colours.

did.unittitle: Image Leather Bandana Color guide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New Jersey

did.unittitle: Business card for S.L.A.M. (Strictly Leather and Men), Hardcore Master & Slave Group

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1347 Folsom St., San Francisco, a South of Market leather bar

did.unittitle: Business card for The Bolt
Various LGBT Cards and Leaflets

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1010 Bryant St., San Francisco. Illustration by A. Jay (aka Al Shapiro, art director of Drummer magazine).

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Hot Piss Pig Party at the Boot Camp Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Polk Gulch was a popular gay neighborhood from the 1950s to the 1980s.

did.unittitle: Handbill for the Polk Gulch Saloon 9 year celebration

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 6th and Folsom, San Francisco

did.unittitle: 2 For 1 Drink Coupon for The Watering Hole

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 401 Sixth St., San Francisco. The End Up opened in November 1973, not as a leather bar but a dance bar, though many leather people who liked to dance went there. It became known for its Jockey Short Dance Contest and achieved further recognition after Armistead Maupin featured it in his weekly newspaper column, Tales of the City.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Valentine's Day Sweetheart Special/Jockey Shorts Dance Contest at The End Up

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oakland. David Kelsey began performing in San Francisco in the mid-1960s, and released an album, Top of the Heap, with Pure Trash in 1983. The Bench and Bar was named by its first owners, a judge and attorney.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing a David Kelsey & Pure Trash fund raiser at the Bench & Bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 975 Harrison St., San Francisco. Illustration by Rex.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Hospital 2 at the Handball Express

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Baybrick Inn (1190 Folsom Street) was a South of Market lesbian guest house, club and bar managed by Lauren Hewitt that operated between 1982 and 1987.

did.unittitle: Baybrick Inn Handbill

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Illustration by Susie Jordan.

did.unittitle: Being part of Baybrick

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1548 Polk St., San Francisco. N'Touch opened in 1974 as the first gay bar for Asians in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Handbill for N'Touch Sundays disco/bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill advertising Couple-Dancing for Gay Men in the Castro area

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 953 Natoma Street, San Francisco. Hal Slate and Stephen Gilman (members of the San Francisco Gay Men's Chorus) opened The Caldron in 1980 in the South of Market area as a gay sex club where patrons were required to be naked except for footwear.

did.unittitle: Membership card for The Caldron

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Baybrick Inn (1190 Folsom Street) was a South of Market lesbian guest house, club and bar managed by Lauren Hewitt that operated between 1982 and 1987.

did.unittitle: Afterhours Gender Bender Offenders handbill, Baybrick Inn

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: All Aboard The Mae Express - Be Gay with Mae

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oakland

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Grand Ol' Party at the Bench & Bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Esta Noche opened in 1979 at 3079 16th Street as the first gay Latino bar in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Halloween Costume Contest at Esta Noche

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 45 Turk St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Halloween Madness at The Landmark

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4086 18th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Brunch at The Village

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 375 11th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing the first South of Market Leather Daddy Contest at Chaps

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 456 Castro St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Mr. Gay San Francisco 1983 at Castro Station

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 398 12th St., San Francisco. The Eagle, now one of the oldest leather bars in San Francisco, opened in the South of Market area in 1981 and became known for hosting barbecues and beer busts to benefit charitable organizations. The San Andreas Motorcycle Club of Southern California was established in 1965 as a gay biker club and held its first Blessing of the Bikes ceremony in the same year.

did.unittitle: Beer Bust organized by the San Andreas Motorcycle Club, Inc. at the SF Eagle

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4149 18th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill for the Pipeline bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Trocadero Transfer was founded by Dick Collier as an after-hours dance club at 520 4th St. in the South of Market area in 1977. (Frank) Loverde was a San Francisco-based disco artist whose record, Die Hard Lover (co-produced with Patrick Cowley), achieved success in 1982.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Mardi Gras II featuring Loverde at the Trocadero

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 375 11th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing the 2nd Annual Leather Daddy Contest at Chaps

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Pier Pressure ("A Night of Non-Stop Dance Classics") at Pier 45

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Men Behind Bars II at the Victoria Theater

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco

did.unittitle: Boys at the Brick (Baybrick Inn)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Amsterdam. The Amsterdam Jacks formed in 1986 in Amsterdam as a response to the threat of AIDS, later expanding to Paris, Cologne and Marseilles as well as twelve cities in the USA.

did.unittitle: Jack-Off Parties of the Amsterdam Jacks
Baybrick Inn September Calendar 1986

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 520 4th St., San Francisco. Club Q opened in 1987 and featured pioneering lesbian DJ Page Hodel.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing A Valentine's Dance for Women and Friends organized by Club Q

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 520 Haight St., San Francisco. A few old tack holes. Mentions DJ Gregory Cruikshank, formerly an early member of Tuxedomoon and, before that, part of The Angels of Light (an offshoot of the Cockettes).

did.unittitle: Handbill for the Fag Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 336 6th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill promoting the new Taste of Leather Erotica Emporium

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 240 Golden Gate, San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Circus, a benefit for Godfather Fund and Aids Emergency Fund

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Special guest Lola Lust (Luis Cesar Cabrera) arrived in San Francisco in 1973 and won the title of Mr. Gay San Francisco in 1979, later becoming a regular headliner at Esta Noche.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Queen of the Universe Beauty Pageant at Esta Noche

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 530 Valencia, San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Kamala and the Karnivores, Blue Vulva Underground and Diamanda Kali-Hagen at the Deaf Club
Handbill announcing a Putting on the Lips Contest at Esta Noche, mc'eed by Lola Lust

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 520 4th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test with NY house star Maurice at Heaven's Gates

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 960 Folsom St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill advertising the Studstore

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 398 12th St., San Francisco

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Men in Uniform Beer Bust at SF Eagle

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1062 Valencia, San Francisco. Carl Linkhart moved to San Francisco in 1973 where he became a member of The Angels of Light and the early Sisters of Perpetual Indulgence, later performing as a cabaret singer and composer under the name Carl with Records.

did.unittitle: Handbill announcing Carl with Records at The Marsh
Series II. Posters
Folsom Street Barracks Hotel - Sauna Baths & Bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Todd Trexler featuring a photograph by Richard Avedon. The Barracks opened in 1972 at 72 Hallam St. (off Folsom) as San Francisco's first leather-oriented bathhouse, catering for people into hardcore BDSM.

did.unittitle: The Pleasure Chest - sex hardware

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York City. Artwork by Rex. The Pleasure Chest opened in 1972 at 183 West 10th Street, NYC, with branches opening later in several other cities.

did.unittitle: The Slot

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Bill Tellman. The Slot, one of the earliest leather-oriented bathhouses in San Francisco, opened on Folsom Street in 1976, catering for those into hardcore flagellation.

did.unittitle: The Magazine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Rex. The Magazine opened in the Spring of 1973, specializing in gay erotica. Its owner, Trent Dunphy, began with the front half of a small shop at 839 Larkin Street, next to The Gangway, San Francisco's oldest gay bar, later moving to larger premises nearby.

did.unittitle: Come West At The Double R Miami

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Florida, The Double R was a Levileather bar in downtown Miami, originally known as the Rack and the Ramrod.

did.unittitle: A Taste of Leather

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Rex. A Taste of Leather, one of the first in-bar leather stores, was established in 1967 by Nick O'Demus above Febe's on Folsom Street.

did.unittitle: Touché

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chicago. Artwork by Cegun. Touché opened as a leather bar at 6412 N. Clark St, Chicago in 1977.

did.unittitle: San Francisco Trucking Company Inc.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Ken Wood.

did.unittitle: VIP Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reno, NV. Artwork by Dirk. Dave Kirkcaldy and Rex Allen opened Reno's first gay owned and operated hotel in 1965, and opened a mixed bar for gay men, lesbians, bisexuals, drag queens and others a year later. In 1975 it was expanded to include a bathhouse.

did.unittitle: Fairoaks calendar, December

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Rinaldo. The Fairoaks hotel was a converted apartment building at the corner of Oak and Steiner owned and operated from 1977 to 1979 by a group of men who had previously lived together in a commune.

did.unittitle: Catacombs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Zach. The Catacombs was an SM club in the South of Market area that grew out of the local Fist Fuckers of America and operated between 1975 and 1981. Its location was semi-secret and admission was by referral only. In March 1980, Cynthia Slater and Susan Thoraen rented the Catacombs for the first mixed gender, mixed orientation SM play party. It reopened at another location from 1982 but closed for good in 1984 in response to the antibathhouse campaign.

did.unittitle: Febe's Eleventh and Folsom Calendar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. 'Leather David' artwork by Mike Caffee. Febe's was the first leather bar on Folsom Street. It opened on July 25th 1966 and was particularly popular with members of gay motorcycle clubs.

did.unittitle: A Taste of Leather Calendar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Rex.

did.unittitle: Hothouse Christmas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Hothouse opened in San Francisco in 1979 on the northwest corner of 5th and Harrison as a BDSM bathhouse.

did.unittitle: Chicago Eagle

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos). The Eagle's Nest, established in New York City in 1970 soon after the Stonewall riots, was the first of what would become an international 'brand' for gay bars. The Eagle became a byword for the hypermasculine leather aesthetic, and at least 50 bars around the world took the name, though there was no formal connection between them.

did.unittitle: Cum For Ride, DAM

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York City. Artwork by Greg Maskwa. Maskwa painted murals for various bathhouses and bars, including the Brig in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: The Jaguar Adult Bookstore and Private Membership Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Ken Wood. The bookstore had a bi-level sex club up- and downstairs.

did.unittitle: The Gym, Houston Muscle & Power Co.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artist unknown.

did.unittitle: The 1980 International Mr. Leather Contest, The Gold Coast

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos). The Gold Coast, cited as America's first leather bar, was founded by Chuck Renslow in 1958 and its International Mr. Leather contest, first held in 1979, is regarded as having turned what was originally a local leather competition into a national lifestyle (John Lunning was the first winner of its predecessor, Mr. Gold Coast, in 1972).

did.unittitle: I Want You At The 247 Bar In Philadelphia

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Designed by Colt Studios. The 247 leather and denim bar opened at 247 S. 17th St., Philadelphia in 1971.

did.unittitle: Looking for Mr. Drummer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Ken Wood. Drummer was founded by John H. Embry and Jeanne Barney in Los Angeles in 1975 and became the most successful American leather magazine. The publication played a major role in promoting gay leather as a lifestyle and masculinity as a gay ideal, and gave birth to the California Motorcycle Club Carnival and the Mr. Drummer contest in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: International Mr. Leather 1983, The Gold Coast

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chicago. Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos).

did.unittitle: Gold Coast 501 N. Clark, Chicago

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos)

did.unittitle: Studstore

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by The Hun. The Studstore opened on the site of A Taste of Leather in October 1983, later becoming a video store.

did.unittitle: Gold Coast 501 N. Clark, Chicago

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos)

did.unittitle: Chicago Gold Coast

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Etienne (Dom Orejudos).

did.unittitle: The Jaguar Adult Bookstore and Private Membership Club

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco.

did.unittitle: Barbary Coasters M/C Warlocks M/C Present A Joint Weekend Run

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Warlocks (southern California) and Barbary Coasters (San Francisco) motorcycle clubs were formed in 1960 and 1966 respectively.

did.unittitle: Watering Hole Saloon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. The Watering Hole (formerly known as The Roundup) first opened on Folsom Street in San Francisco as a meeting place for urolagniacs in 1978.

did.unittitle: Lone Star Saloon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Rex.

did.unittitle: Lone Star Saloon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artwork by Steve Stafford. Poster for the 'Second Opening' (following the 1989 Loma Prieta Earthquake the original Lone Star Saloon moved from Howard Street to Harrison/9th).

did.unittitle: Rawhide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Rex. The Levileather bar opened in 1979 on 8th Ave at 21st St. in Chelsea, NYC.

did.unittitle: Rawhide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York City

did.unittitle: Rawhide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York City

did.unittitle: Manhole

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by The Hun. Manhole opened in the Boystown neighborhood of Chicago in October 1990 and was Halsted Street's first leather bar, known for its DJs and cutting-edge music.

did.unittitle: The Stud Bar

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Design and photography by David Jude. The Stud bar opened in 1966 at 1535 Folsom St. as, according to co-founder George Matson, a "bar for people, not just pretty bodies". It was originally a Hell's Angels hangout and subsequently became a dance bar for hippies on the margins of the leather scene. It moved to 399 9th St. at Harrison in 1987.

did.unittitle: Mr. Alberta Drummer '92

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Club Calgary.

did.unittitle: Cissy's Saloon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Artist unknown. Cissy's Saloon opened as a lesbian bar at 1590 Folsom Street in 1972.

did.unittitle: Series III. Clothing
collection of buttons, badges and drink tokens from various leather bars and clubs
collection of buttons and badges from various biker clubs throughout the USA

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Including the Satyrs, the first gay motorcycle club in the United States (founded in Los Angeles in 1954), the Wheels MC (founded in New York City in 1969), and the CMC Carnival (California Motorcycle Club Carnival). The CMCC, a gay leather BDSM dance, was organized by one of the earliest leather biker clubs, the California Motorcycle Club, and held every November between 1966 and 1986 at various indoor venues in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: collection of pins from various gay biker clubs throughout the USA,

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes pins from the South Pacific MC, founded in Sydney in 1970.

did.unittitle: Vintage leatherman's biker cap

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Adorned with an ssortment of motorcycle club badges dating from the late 1960s through to the early 1980s.

did.unittitle: T-shirt from the Lone Star Saloon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: San Francisco. Screenprinted artwork by Steve Stafford.
